terms.def: Active return / tracking error; the Sharpe ratio of active management. IR > 0.5 is considered good for long-only.
Portfolio return − benchmark return (alpha); the excess return the manager must explain.
Std dev of active returns; how much the portfolio deviates from the benchmark.
Correlation between return forecasts and actual returns; measures skill per individual bet.
Number of independent active bets per year; more bets = more opportunities to express skill.
IR = IC × √BR (Grinold 1989); high IR requires both skill (IC) and many opportunities (BR).

eli3: The Information Ratio (IR) measures an active manager's ability to generate excess returns above a benchmark per unit of active risk taken. IR = Active Return / Tracking Error = (Portfolio Return - Benchmark Return) / std(Active Return). It's the Sharpe ratio of active management — a high IR means the manager consistently adds value without taking excessive deviation from the benchmark.

A manager who beats their benchmark by 3% per year with 2% tracking error has an IR of 1.5 — exceptional. A manager who beats by 3% per year with 6% tracking error has IR of 0.5 — modest. The IR rewards consistency: a small, steady outperformance is better than a large, erratic one because investors are penalised by benchmark-relative underperformance (career risk).

The Fundamental Law of Active Management (Grinold 1989) connects IR to the manager's skill and breadth: IR ≈ IC × √BR, where IC is the Information Coefficient (correlation between predictions and outcomes) and BR is the number of independent bets. This says breadth and skill are equally important — a manager with modest skill but many independent bets can achieve a high IR.

whenWhy.title: IR in fund evaluation
whenWhy.body: Standard thresholds: IR > 0.5 = good; IR > 0.75 = very good; IR > 1.0 = exceptional; rare to sustain above 1.5. Why? The Fundamental Law limits most managers: a typical long-only equity manager has IC ≈ 0.05 (modest skill) and BR ≈ 300 independent positions per year. IR ≈ 0.05 × √300 ≈ 0.87. To achieve IR > 1.5: need either higher IC (very skilled manager) or more breadth (more positions, more asset classes). Comparison across managers: IR is more comparable across managers with different risk profiles than absolute return. A manager with IR = 0.8 at 2% TE is better than a manager with IR = 0.5 at 5% TE, even though both beat the benchmark. Stability: IR computed over a 3-year track record has high statistical uncertainty. Standard error: SE(IR) ≈ √(1+IR²/2) / √T ≈ 0.25 for T = 36 months and IR = 0.5. A '95% CI' spans from -0 to 1.0 — so 3 years is barely sufficient to confirm even moderate skill. Minimum track record for statistical confidence: 5-10 years.

whenWhy.title: Fundamental Law of Active Management
whenWhy.body: Grinold's Law: IR ≈ IC × √BR. IC = Information Coefficient: rank correlation between manager's forecasts and actual outcomes. IC of 0.05 is good for equity; 0.1-0.15 is excellent. BR = Breadth: number of independent investment decisions per year. For a stock picker with 50 positions: BR ≈ 50 (if annual rebalancing). For a quantitative fund making daily factor bets: BR = 252 × N_assets. The √BR scaling: adding independent bets improves IR proportionally to their square root — the law of large numbers. Practical implications: (1) Diversification improves IR: more positions → higher BR → higher IR for same IC; (2) Transaction costs reduce BR: each trade costs real money, reducing net IC; (3) Short selling increases BR: by taking both long and short positions, a manager doubles their bets; explains the higher IR of market-neutral vs. long-only funds; (4) Multi-asset diversification: a manager who makes independent bets across equities, bonds, currencies, and commodities has much higher BR than a single-asset manager — key advantage of global macro and systematic macro funds.

whenWhy.title: IR in portfolio construction and allocation
whenWhy.body: The IR is the optimal alpha-to-risk ratio in the following sense: if a manager has IR = μ_α / σ_TE, the optimal active risk budget (TE) that maximises the Sharpe ratio of the total portfolio (benchmark + active) is: TE* = (IR / λ) = (μ_α/σ_TE) / λ, where λ is the fund's risk aversion coefficient. Higher IR → larger active risk budget is optimal. This framework is used by pension funds to allocate active risk across managers: a manager with IR = 0.8 at 4% TE should receive a larger active allocation than a manager with IR = 0.4 at 4% TE. In multi-manager portfolios: optimal weights are proportional to each manager's IR (similar to Sharpe ratio weighting in asset allocation). An aggregate IR across multiple uncorrelated managers: IR_portfolio ≈ √Σ IR²_i — diversification of active risk improves aggregate IR.

deepDiveIntro: The information ratio equals mean active return divided by the standard deviation of active return, both measured over the same period and annualized consistently. Its t-statistic equals the IR multiplied by the square root of the number of years of data, so an IR of 0.5 over five years is not statistically significant. Confirming manager skill typically requires twenty or more years. The IR differs from the Sharpe ratio in that it measures return relative to a benchmark rather than to the risk-free rate; the two coincide only when the benchmark earns the risk-free rate.
formula: IR = μ_A / σ_A;   t-stat = IR × √T

deepDive.heading: Modified Fundamental Law: Incorporating Transfer Coefficient
deepDive.body: Clarke, de Silva and Thorley (2002) extended the Fundamental Law to include the Transfer Coefficient, which measures how efficiently a manager translates signals into actual portfolio positions. Constraints such as long-only requirements, position limits, and turnover caps prevent full expression of negative signals, reducing the transfer coefficient below one. A long-only manager typically has a transfer coefficient around 0.65, while a long-short manager can achieve 0.9 or higher. This gap in transfer coefficient largely explains why market-neutral funds tend to achieve higher information ratios than equivalent long-only strategies with the same signal quality. [1]

deepDive.heading: Ex-Ante vs Ex-Post IR
deepDive.body: The ex-ante information ratio is the expected ratio estimated before trading begins, based on signal validation data, assumed breadth, and portfolio construction efficiency. The ex-post IR is the ratio realized from actual returns. In practice the ex-post IR typically falls well below the ex-ante figure because signals lose predictive power over time, positions are correlated so effective breadth is lower than assumed, and transaction costs reduce net signal quality. Projecting IR from a paper portfolio without accounting for real-world costs often overstates live performance by twenty to forty percent. [2]

deepDive.heading: IR Persistence and Manager Selection
deepDive.body: Over short horizons of one to three years, information ratio persistence is weak — past winners are only marginally more likely to outperform than other managers. Persistence is stronger over ten or more years for managers with structural, repeatable sources of alpha, and for systematic factor strategies where the premium itself is durable. From an allocation standpoint, track records shorter than five years provide little statistical confidence, and net IR after fees is the relevant number — many managers with a gross IR of 0.5 deliver near-zero net IR after a two-and-twenty fee structure. [1]

takeaways: IR = Active Return / Tracking Error; thresholds: 0.5 = good, 0.75 = very good, 1.0 = exceptional. [1]
Fundamental Law: IR ≈ IC × √BR; breadth and skill are equally important. [1]
Modified law: IR ≈ TC × IC × √BR; long-only constraint reduces TC to ~0.65, lowering IR vs. long-short. [1]
Statistical significance: t-stat = IR × √T; need 20+ years for IR = 0.5 to reach 95% confidence. [2]
IR persistence: weak at 1-3 years; stronger for factor-based and systematic strategies. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why can IR be negative even if the manager has positive gross returns?
edgeCases.a: IR measures active return vs. benchmark, not absolute return. A manager can have positive absolute returns but negative IR if: (1) The benchmark returned more than the manager (e.g., manager +8%, S&P 500 +15% in a bull market); (2) The manager's active return is negative (they underperformed the benchmark) on average. This is very common: in bull markets, value managers typically have positive absolute returns but negative IR because their 'cheap' stocks underperform the bull market's growth leaders. Example: 2015-2019, US value equity managers averaged +6%/year absolute, but the S&P 500 returned +12%/year. IR = (6% - 12%) / TE = negative. This is why IR is the correct benchmark for active management evaluation — absolute returns reflect both the market beta (which can be obtained cheaply from an index fund) and the active contribution.

edgeCases.q: How does IR change when you run the same strategy across multiple markets?
edgeCases.a: Applying the same strategy (same IC) across N independent markets multiplies breadth by N: IR_multi = IC × √(BR × N) = √N × IR_single. A momentum strategy with IC = 0.05 and BR = 100 per market, run across 5 independent markets: IR = 0.05 × √500 ≈ 1.12 vs. single market IR = 0.05 × √100 ≈ 0.5. This is the diversification benefit of global multi-asset strategies — the same signal quality (IC) generates higher IR when applied across more markets. Key caveat: the markets must be truly independent (correlation of factor returns ≈ 0). If the factor has high cross-market correlation (global momentum tends to be correlated internationally), the effective BR is much less than N × BR_single. The actual IR improvement is √(1 + (N-1) × (1-ρ)) × IR_single, where ρ is the cross-market signal correlation. At ρ = 0.5, adding a second market improves IR by only √1.5 ≈ 1.22×, not √2 = 1.41×.

sources.label: Grinold, R.C. (1989) — The Fundamental Law of Active Management, JPM
Clarke, R., de Silva, H. & Thorley, S. (2002) — Portfolio Constraints and the Fundamental Law, FAJ
